title: Predictive Three-Dimensional Printing of Spare Parts with Internet of Things
abstract: Industry 4.0 integrates digital and physical technologies to transform work management, where two core enablers are the internet of things (IoT) and three-dimensional printing (3DP). IoT monitors complex systems in real time, whereas 3DP enables agile manufacturing that can respond to real-time information. However, the details of how these two can be integrated are not yet clear. To gain insights, we consider a scenario where a three-dimensional (3D) printer supplies a critical part to multiple machines that are embedded with sensors and connected through IoT. Although the public perception indicates that this integration would enable on-demand printing, our research suggests that this is not necessarily the case. Instead, the true benefit is the ability to print predictively. In particular, it is typically more effective for the 3D printer to predictively print to stock based on a threshold that depends on the system’s status. We also identify a printing mode called predictive print on demand that allows for minimal inventory, and we find the speed of 3DP to be the primary factor that influences its optimality. Furthermore, we assess the value of IoT in cost reductions by separately analyzing the impact of advance information from embedded sensors and the real-time information fusion through IoT. We find that IoT provides significant value in general. However, the conventional wisdom that IoT’s value scales up for larger systems is suitable only when the expansion is paired with appropriate 3DP capacity. Our framework can help inform investment decisions regarding IoT/embedded sensors and support the development of scheduling tools for predictive 3DP.

author.Jing-Sheng Song: Fuqua School of Business, Duke University, Durham, North Carolina 27708
author.Yue Zhang: Smeal College of Business, Pennsylvania State University, University Park, Pennsylvania 16802

article.1. Introduction: 1.1. Motivation Industry 4.0 heralds a new era of industrial revolution, which is centered around a range of technologies that enable the integration of digital and physical domains. This paradigm has the potential to transform the current approach to information utilization and work management. One prominent example is the internet of things (IoT), which “describes physical objects (or groups of such objects) with sensors, processing ability, software, and other technologies that connect and exchange data with other devices and systems over the internet or other communications networks” (Wikipedia 2023). The economic impact of IoT is estimated to be in the trillions of dollars by 2025, primarily because of the operational applications in inventory management, maintenance, worker productivity, and optimization opportunities (McKinsey 2015). Against this backdrop, this paper proposes an analytical framework to study the impact of IoT using the context of optimizing spare parts supply for machine maintenance. Embedded sensors are not new inventions; they estimate the operating condition of machines using their measurements (temperature, vibration level, etc.) and inference models (Saghafian et al. 2021). Indeed, condition-based maintenance (CBM) of operational assets leveraging embedded sensors has received vast practical and academic attention, long predating the emergence of IoT (Wijnmalen and Hontelez 1997). Without IoT, data from embedded sensors are isolated. Sensors work separately based on their own readings, such as generating an alert when maintenance is due soon (Banner 2023). However, the ubiquitous and connected sensors in IoT coupled with advanced predictive analytics are making CBM a key IoT application area (McKinsey 2015). The real-time connectivity enables the decision-making-levelinformation fusion(Xu and Xu 2017), with rich local information (sensor data) dynamically translated into system-level information. As a result, IoT enables a dynamic representation of a complex system or thedigital twinknown as “a sensor-enabled digital model of a physical object that simulates the object in a live setting” (Parrott and Warshaw 2017). To take advantage of the real-time information offered by IoT, it naturally calls for the capability to act timely and flexibly. Three-dimensional printing (3DP)—also known as additive manufacturing—is a flexible production technology in Industry 4.0. Among the many industries that 3DP may influence, the spare parts business appears to be particularly promising. For instance, it has “proven to be a viable option” for producing “strategic replacement parts such as gas turbine nozzles, pump manifolds, rotors, sealing accessories, and more,” and a French oil and gas company “was able to limit drilling rig downtime by [three-dimensional] printing repair parts locally and bypassing expensive logistics and shipping costs” (Hayford 2023). According to a survey of 38 major suppliers and buyers of spare parts in Germany (Geissbauer et al. 2017), “this additive manufacturing technology … will enable suppliers to make and send parts on an on-demand basis—and do so locally, close to where the parts are needed.”Print on demandmeans that a printing job is initiated upon the arrival of a demand. It eliminates inventory by storing parts in a virtual warehouse (as digital files) rather than on a shelf, and it is in contrast toprint to stock, where printing jobs are for inventory replenishment. However, print on demand also shuts out any immediate availability. The printing time, varying from hours to a couple of days at least, is nonnegligible. With IoT, 3DP can be carried out in a predictive way by leveraging the real-time information from IoT. How to optimally manage the predictive 3DP process, however, remains unclear. Depending on whether a printing job is triggered upon anticipating an upcoming demand or to replenish inventory, the printing process can be eitherpredictive print on demandorpredictive print to stock. Compared with print on demand, predictive print on demand advances the timing of printing jobs. As a result, a printed part can be held as inventory during predictive print on demand if the printing is finished before the arrival of the anticipated demand. A summary of predictive 3DP policies is provided inFigure 1. For spare parts suppliers facing investment decisions on IoT, an in-depth understanding of its impact on their existing practice is crucial. According to Dave Rauch, Senior Vice President of Drive Manufacturing at Western Digital, “[t]he first step is recognizing the potential value of IoT. Then you can commit to a road map that allows the organization to make the transition” (Teachey 2021). The goal of this research is to pave the way to understanding the structure of the optimal predictive printing policy and to enable the assessment of IoT’s value. 1.2. Framework and Findings We consider the context of spare parts replacement service for a single type of critical part used in multiple machines. The part supply source is a three-dimensional (3D) printer with limited capacity, the machines are embedded with sensors, and the entire system (the printer and the machines) are connected through IoT. The sensors continuously monitor the degradation of parts, and this information is centrally available to support a predictive printing decision that specifies when to print a part, now or later. The objective is to minimize the total discounted spare parts inventory holding and back-order cost. We study the following research questions. First, what is the optimal predictive printing policy? Second, when is predictive print on demand optimal, and when is additional inventory needed (i.e., predictive print to stock)? Third, how can the value of IoT in enabling predictive 3DP be assessed? To address thefirstquestion, we construct anintelligent system(Section 3.2.1) that leverages IoT (and thus, embedded sensors), allowing the 3D printer to predictively print parts based on the real-time system status. However, analyzing this production-inventory system presents a unique challenge as the spare parts demand generated from machines is endogenous. This means that a failed machine without an available replacement part will remain in failure and generate no further demand until it is replaced, and the availability of replacement supply, in turn, is affected by the printing policy. As a result, obtaining a complete characterization of the optimal policy for the general intelligent system is intractable. Nonetheless, we are able to obtain complete characterization of the optimal policy for some special systems and partial characterization for the general intelligent system. Our analyses reveal that it is always optimal to print when there are failed parts. When all machines are operational, we demonstrate that it is optimal to print up to a stationary base-stock level if the part lifetime is exponential and up to a system lifetime status-dependent base-stock level if the system size is one and the part lifetime has two phases (Section 4.2). We find that the optimal base-stock level increases as the system size grows in the former case (Section 4.2.1) and as the functioning part ages in the latter case (Section 4.2.2). These analytical results represent a step forward in advancing the theory as the optimal policy for the production-inventory systems with endogenous demand considered here has not been established in the literature, even for exponential lifetimes. Finally, we numerically verify the optimality of the identified policy for general intelligent systems (Section 7). We address the analytical challenge posed by the general intelligent system by introducing anupper-bound systemthat assumes the availability of an emergency source for parts borrowing to deal with exogenous demand. We prove the general optimality of the system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy for this system (Section 5.1), which enables the generalization of insights obtained from analyzing special intelligent systems. Our analyses show that the aging of parts, leading to imminent replacement demands, results in predictive printing orders and that the optimal printing decisions are determined by system-level (rather than part-level) criteria (Section 5.2). We establish that this system serves as an upper bound for the intelligent system in terms of optimal costs and base-stock levels (Section 5.2), and its optimal policy can be an effective heuristic for the intelligent system, with an average cost error of only 0.01% (Section 7). Furthermore, we demonstrate through a queueing perspective that this system approximates the intelligent system well by preserving the dependence of the optimal policy on cost terms (Section 5.2.1). To answer thesecondquestion, our analyses show that the optimality of predictive print on demand becomes less likely as the system size increases (Sections 4.2.1and5.1). In our numerical study, we further investigate the factors affecting the occurrence of predictive print on demand as optimal (Section 7). We find that the most significant factor is the printing speed, which can cause a wide variation in the occurrence—ranging from 16.67% to 92.71% as the unit printing time decreases from one week to 1.2 days. In comparison, the impact of the secondary factor, the unit printing cost, is much smaller; as it increases from $100 to $1,000, the occurrence varies from 31.25% to 68.75%. This suggests that the optimality of predictive print on demand, which allows for minimal inventory, depends heavily on the acceleration of the 3DP speed. Otherwise, even with IoT, predictive print to stock would still be preferred. To address thethirdquestion, we introduce two benchmark systems (Section 3.2.2) that have information structures predating the IoT era: thepassive systemwithout IoT or embedded sensors and theproactive systemwith embedded sensors but no IoT. In the passive system, the 3D printer responds passively, triggering printing orders only when part failures occur (Section 6.1). In the proactive system, the 3D printer can print proactively based on the advance orders generated by disconnected sensors (Section 6.2). By comparing the intelligent system against these benchmarks, we find that the positive impact of IoT hinges upon the effectiveness of embedded sensors in providing nontrivial estimates of the remaining part lifetimes. We then develop metrics to assess the value of IoT in cost reductions (Section 6.3), and we show that its value can be decomposed into a portion attributed to the advance information from embedded sensors and another attributed to IoT’s real-time information fusion. These composite values can be used to determine whether investing in IoT or embedded sensors alone is worthwhile. We further implement the metrics in a numerical study (Section 7), and we find that IoT provides substantial value in general, resulting in relative cost reductions of up to 77.40% and an average of 24.56%. However, our findings also suggest that IoT’s value does not necessarily scale up in the number of machines unless the 3DP capacity is paired appropriately. Therefore, caution is needed when executing the conventional wisdom that IoT’s value scales up for larger systems. Additionally, we observe a consistent pattern in the composition of IoT’s value across all instance subsets, with the portion attributed to advance information always predominating that of real-time information fusion. An overview of our three-layer framework is provided inFigure 2.
article.2. Literature Review: There has been growing research interest in how Industry 4.0 technologies, such as IoT and 3DP, can be leveraged to improve operations management; see, for example,Chen et al. (2019,2023) andOlsen and Tomlin (2020).Saghafian et al. (2021)study how sensors can improve their own estimates by selecting and soliciting estimates from other sensors. Their emphasis is not on operational decision making, but their results can be used as an input to support those actions.Westerweel et al. (2019)study age-based preventive maintenance for a single part in a single machine using 3DP as the additional option to the regular replenishment of spare parts. They assume on-demand 3DP with an uncapacitated constant lead time, and the printed parts have inferior quality. They also assume a storage limit of one unit for regular parts.Song and Zhang (2020)consider multiple distinctive parts and focus on the sourcing strategy for each part between two options: procuring to stock from a traditional manufacturer with an exogenous lead time or print on demand using a 3D printer with finite capacity. In the current paper, we study the replacement service for a single part used inmultiplemachines connected via IoT. A 3D printer with a finite capacity is the main source for replacement part supply. We investigate the optimal control policy for the 3D printer. Quality concerns or other trade-offs with nonprinted parts are not considered. Within the extensive literature of spare parts inventory management, our work is mostly related to optimizing inventory management and part replacement schedule.Armstrong and Atkins (1996)consider a simple system with one component. The ordering quantity is always one with a constant lead time. The focus is to derive the optimal replacement and inventory ordering times.Panagiotidou (2020)considers the joint optimization of parts ordering and age-based preventive replacement. An age-independent(s,S) policy is adopted for inventory control with constant lead time and lost sales (unmet demand is satisfied by an emergency source). See the references inPanagiotidou (2020)for a more comprehensive review of the relevant literature.Deshpande et al. (2006)take maintenance scheduling as given and focus on inventory decisions based on part ages. They link the maintenance database with the inventory database from the U.S. Coast Guard and propose replenishment policies based on the empirical observations. They find that policies accounting for part age can lead to significant reductions in inventory cost.Eruguz et al. (2018)consider a single part used in a single moving asset and model the degradation of the part as a continuous-time Markov chain modulated by the operational process of the moving asset. Assuming part storage to be at most one unit, they show that the optimal replacement and inventory ordering policy follows a bithreshold structure. We also take replacement scheduling as given and focus on the optimal control of a capacitated 3D printer in supplying the part. The information provided by IoT allows our control policy to depend on part age. Many authors have studied advance demand information; see, for example,Angelus and Özer (2016)and the references therein. Within this literature, our work is related most to the stream that leverages condition-based monitoring to improve inventory and spare parts replacement decisions.Elwany and Gebraeel (2008)extend upon the simple model inArmstrong and Atkins (1996)by replacing the fixed lifetime distribution with sensor-driven remaining lifetime distributions that dynamically evolve (via Bayesian updating) according to the degradation states of components. They demonstrate that incorporating sensor data can significantly reduce replacement and inventory costs.Li and Ryan (2011)consider a periodic-review finite-horizon inventory model for a single part used in multiple machines, where part demand distribution is Bayesian updated leveraging the degradation signals observed. They assume uncapacitated supply of parts with zero replenishment lead time and show that a degradation signal-dependent base-stock policy is optimal.Lin et al. (2017)also consider a single part used in multiple machines but in a periodic-review, infinite-horizon model with uncapacitated supply and constant lead time. They derive properties of the optimal policy and propose three heuristics. Our paper differs from these works in several ways. First, capacitated 3DP is the main source of supply. Second, similar toLin et al. (2017)andEruguz et al. (2018), we model the lifetime of a part as a series of phases and represent the degradation process as the transition of lifetime status from one phase to its successor. This treatment enables us to isolate and quantify the values of monitoring technologies. Third, we consider a continuous-review, infinite-horizon model. Our work is also related to the production-inventory literature on make-to-stock (MTS)/make-to-order (MTO) queues, where the supply source is a production facility with finite capacity. Some works consider multiple different products and study either which products to MTS and which to MTO or the priority rule in production scheduling. See, for example,Arreola-Risa and DeCroix (1998),Rajagopalan (2002),Youssef et al. (2004), and the references therein.Gupta and Benjaafar (2004)consider MTS single items in the first stage of production and MTO differentiated items in the second stage of production.Ha (2000)considers the rationing of a single product with multiple demand classes. In contrast, we consider an identical part used in multiple machines supplied by a 3D printer. Instead of the exogenous demand considered in this literature, the replacement demand in our system is endogenous. We propose predictive print on demand to leverage the condition monitoring information collected via IoT. This new mode is in between MTS and MTO, and it is a refined concept of print on demand (i.e., MTO) as advocated in industry. We examine when predictive print on demand is optimal.
article.3. Model Framework: In this section, we introduce the basic model setting and also provide an overview of our analyses. 3.1. Model Basics We consider an original equipment manufacturer (OEM) that offers after-sales replacement service of a critical part formmachines sold to its customers. Each machine uses one unit of this part; hence, all the parts in use can be indexed byj∈M≔{1,…,m}. The OEM uses a 3D printer to supply the replacement parts. 3.1.1. Part Lifetime and Replacement Dynamics Part Lifetime. Because the parts are identical and are used in separate machines, we assume that their lifetimes are independent of each other and have an identical phase-type distribution withkphases, where phasei’s duration is exponentially distributed with meanνi−1,i∈K≔{1,…,k}. These features are commonly adopted in the spare parts literature (see, e.g.,Muckstadt 2005). The parameterskandνican be estimated from historical sensor data (Muriel et al. 2018). Note that ifk= 1, the part lifetime is exponentially distributed and thus, memoryless (Wolff 1989). As a result, the estimate for the remaining lifetime of a part is always the same as that of a new part. Although this can be the true lifetime distribution for some parts, this distribution can also be used to model the situation in which the sensors are too noisy to provide informative data (seeSection 3.1.2). Replacement Dynamics. When a part fails, a replacement is due immediately and fulfilled from the spare parts inventory if available. We defer introducing the logistics for stockout until later as it is the key difference between the intelligent system and the upper-bound system. The part replacement here can accommodate both corrective and preventive replacement by appropriately defining the lifetimes of parts. Specifically, although we use the term “failure” to describe the status of a part when a replacement is due, it need not be the actual failure. A corrective replacement will take place if it is indeed an actual failure; otherwise, it can refer to the failure as defined in degradation modeling when the degradation signal reaches a predetermined failure threshold such that a preventive replacement is due (see, e.g.,Elwany and Gebraeel 2008). Without loss of generality, we assume that the replacement time is negligible—another common assumption in the literature (Panagiotidou 2020). 3.1.2. Embedded Sensors. Sensorestimates can be noisy and cannot accurately predict the remaining lifetime of a part, but they can indicate which phase the part is in its life cycle, with the duration of the phase being uncertain (seeSection 3.1.1). In particular, for partjat timet, the embedded sensor records If sensor estimates are too noisy to provide additional information other than whether a part is functioning or not, they will indicate the same phase for all functioning parts (i.e.,K={1}andYj1(t)=1if partjis functioning at timet). In this case, the alert mechanism is trivial withτ= 1, and the number of parts whose lifetimes have entered or passed the threshold phase is the same as the number of functioning parts (i.e.,∑j∈M∑i∈K,i≥τYji(t)=∑j∈MYj1(t)). 3.1.3. IoT Features. With IoT, at any timet, the sensor records of parts lifetime status in allmmachines{Yji(t),j∈M,i∈K}are known and translated into the digital twin of thesystem lifetime status: 3.1.4. 3DP Features Decentralized and Local Production. Because of the convenience of transmitting and storing digitized part files, 3DP allows for decentralized and local production. Once a part is printed, the transportation time is negligible. Hence, we focus on one 3D printer that is in charge of printing spare parts for multiple sold machines in a local region. Finite Capacity. We assume that the printing time of each part follows aκ-Erlang distribution with mean1/μ, which enables the problem to be cast as a continuous-time Markov decision process, allowing for more fruitful analysis. Although the actual printing time is relatively deterministic because of the automatic feature of the technology, the postprocessing time (not explicitly modeled here and thus, should be included in the “printing time”) usually possesses uncertainty because of the inconsistency in printing quality and the manual efforts often required, contributing to the unpredictability of the actual completion time for a ready-to-use part. For a more general printing time distribution, one can always reasonably approximate it with an Erlang distribution. For ease of exposition, we focus on exponential printing time (i.e.,κ= 1) in the main body of paper, and we demonstrate in Online Appendix A that our analyses and results also carry over to the general Erlang printing time with a much larger state space. Flexibility. With digitized part files in place, the setup cost of a 3DP job is negligible, enabling the flexibility of small-scale productions. 3.2. Overview of Our Approach To answer our research questions, we build several systems depending on whether embedded sensors or IoT features are present. For all systems, a holding cost ratehapplies for each unit of part inventory. Penalty cost applies when a replacement demand finds no on-hand inventory; we defer the details to the introduction of specific systems inSections 3.2.1and5. For each system, the objective is to minimize the expected total discounted system cost. Throughout the paper, we will use the following notations. LetZbe the set of integers,Z+be the set of nonnegative integers, andZ+kbe thek-dimensional crossproducts withZ+1=Z+. Fori=1,…,k, leteibe thek-dimensional vector with one in positioniand zeros elsewhere. Lete0be thek-dimensional vector of zeros, and defineek+1=e0. For any subsetA⊆M, we writegA=(gi,i∈A)as the subvector ofgrestricted to setA. For any vectorg, we writeg¯as the summation of all components. All the proofs can be found in Online Appendix B. 3.2.1. Intelligent System. The system of our primary interest is theintelligent system, in which all parts in use are embedded with built-in sensors and are connected with the 3D printer via IoT. Hence, the status of all parts is continuously monitored and known by the 3D printer. If a part fails when spare parts inventory is depleted, the replacement request is backlogged until a part becomes available. The resulting delay is penalized at a cost rate ofbfor each overdue replacement. The state of the intelligent system at timetconsists of the inventory level of spare partsX(t)∈Z+and the system lifetime statusY(t)as given by (2). Lety=(y1,…,yk)denote a realization ofY(t). Then, the state space is Note thatX(t)·(m−Y¯(t))≡0(i.e., all parts in use must be functioning if there is positive inventory, and the inventory must have been depleted if there are failed parts not yet replaced). Consider an example withm= 4 andk= 3 (i.e., there are four parts in use with three phases in their lifetimes). Then, a system lifetime status ofy=(4,0,0)indicates a safe scenario with all four parts in use being brand new in phase 1, whereas a system lifetime status ofy=(0,1,2)indicates a more urgent scenario with one part being moderately worn (in phase 2), two parts about to fail (in phase 3), and one part in failure (m−y¯=1). Figure 3provides an illustration of the intelligent system. In each state, two actions are possible: print or idle. A deterministic stationary policyπ≔{π(x,y):(x,y)∈S}specifies the action taken at any time as a function of system state only, whereπ(x,y)=1means to print in state(x,y)and whereπ(x,y)=0means not to print in this state. Let the set of such printing policies be denoted by Π. The inventory holding and backlog penalty cost rate when the system is in state(x,y)is Our objective is to find a policy that minimizes the optimal total discounted cost: that is, 3.2.2. Benchmark Systems. To enable a quantification of the impact of IoT, we devise two benchmark systems, where the system lifetime status is not fully observable as in the intelligent system. We introduce them here, and we defer the discussion of policies and metrics toSection 6. Passive System. Neither embedded sensors nor IoT are present. As a result, no predictive printing based on system lifetime status is possible, and replacement demands are generated from part failures passively. Therefore, at any time with system lifetime statusy, the only available information is the number of failed partsm−y¯or equivalently, the number of functioning partsy¯.Figure 4illustrates the passive system. Proactive System. Embedded sensors are present, but there is no IoT. Each sensor works separately based on an integrated alert mechanism described by a thresholdτ∈K(see, e.g.,Banner 2023). Whenever a part’s lifetime status enters phaseτ, an advance order is placed at the 3D printer, which is not fulfilled until when the part fails. LetT≔{τ,τ+1,…,k}. At any time with system lifetime statusy, in addition to the number of functioning partsy¯indicated by part failures, the available information also includes the number of parts in or past phaseτ(i.e.,y¯T≔∑i∈Tyi), which is implied by the number of advance orders.Figure 5illustrates the proactive system.
article.4. Optimal Policy for the Intelligent Sy: InSection 4, we formulate a dynamic program to study the optimal policy for the intelligent system. 4.1. Dynamic Programming Formulation We will work with a uniformized version of the problem so that the transition times0=t0≤t1≤t2≤…are such that{tn+1−tn:n≥0}is a sequence of independent and identically distributed exponential random variables, each with meanΛ−1, whereΛ≔μ+m∑i=1kνi. We normalize Λ to be one without loss of generality (this can be done by redefiningμandνiasμ/Λandνi/Λ). Let{(Xn,Yn):n≥0}denote the embedded Markov chain of states (i.e.,(Xn,Yn)≔(X(tn),Y(tn))is the state immediately after thenth transition), and letXandYdenote its steady-state distribution. If actiona∈{0,1}is selected in state(x,y), then the next state of the embedded Markov chain is(x′,y′)with probability Letγ≔β+1. The optimal expected total discounted cost is LetVbe the set of real-valued functions onS, and letvbe an arbitrary element ofV. DefineΔxv(x,y)≔v(x+1,y)−v(x,y). DefineTi,Tμ:V→Vas follows: The operatorTi:i=1,…,k−1captures a part’s lifetime moving from phaseito phasei+ 1. The operatorTkcaptures a part’s failure with a replacement due, and the operatorTμcaptures the printing decision. Also, consider the operatorT:V→Vdefined by A detailed derivation for (11) is provided in Online Appendix A. In (11),v(x,y)represents the cost to go of being in state(x,y). In particular, it is optimal to print if the cost to go generated from printing a partv(x+𝟙{y¯=m},y+e1𝟙{y¯<m})is less than that of doing nothingv(x,y). Otherwise, it is optimal to idle. The other term∑i=1kνi·(m−yi)v(x,y)in (11) captures the null transitions introduced through uniformization of the jump rate. 4.2. Optimal Policy The functionv*defined in (7) is the minimum nonnegative solution to the optimality equation Moreover, a stationary policy that specifies for each state(x,y)an action that attains the minimum on the right-hand side of (12) is optimal; see, for example,Bertsekas (2017). The stationary policy with printing decision specified by (10) is optimal, and the following intuitive result holds forπ*. It is optimal to print whenever there are parts in failure without replacements(i.e.,for anyy∈Z+k(m−1),we haveπ*(0,y)=1). ByLemma 1, the optimal policy is trivial (always to print) when there are failed parts not replaced yet. As a result, we only need to characterize the nontrivial printing decisions for the states(x,y)withy¯=m(i.e., when allmparts are functioning). In light of this, a system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy for an intelligent system can be described by specifying the base-stock levels for all the system lifetime status inZ+k(m)\Z+k(m−1)={y∈Z+k:y¯=m}. In other words, such a policy can be represented as{sy,y∈Z+k(m)\Z+k(m−1)}, wheresydenotes the base-stock level fory. Note that the policy is predictive print on demand if and only ifminy∈Z+k(m)\Z+k(m−1){sy}=0,and that is, a print order is placednotto replenish inventory but to fulfill an upcoming demand. Otherwise, it is predictive print to stock. In the following, we characterize the optimal policy for the intelligent system. To mark the system under consideration, we apply subscripts when necessary in the form of “m,k.” For example,S1,2,v1,2*, andπ1,2*refer to the state space, value function, and optimal policy, respectively, for the intelligent system withm= 1 andk= 2. 4.2.1. Exponential Lifetime. We first consider the case when the part lifetime is exponentially distributed (i.e.,k= 1). We investigate the role of IoT in connectingmmachines to the 3D printer. The state space (3) is nowSm,1≔Z+×Z+(m), wherey∈Z+(m)represents the number of functioning parts. The transition probability (6) simplifies to LetVm,1be the set of real-valued functions onSm,1, and letvbe an arbitrary element ofVm,1. DefineΔyv(x,y)≔v(x,y+1)−v(x,y)fory∈Z+(m−1). We can show the following properties for the value functionvm,1*. For exponentially distributed part lifetime(i.e.,k= 1),vm,1*∈Um,1≔{v∈Vm,1:vsatisfiesproperties(13)–(17)},where the properties are Proposition 1implies the structural properties of the optimal policyπm,1*. In particular, (13) states a convexity property in inventory level that when allmparts are working, the value (i.e., cost reduction) of having one extra unit of inventory decreases in inventory level. (14) and (15) jointly describe the convexity structure of the value function in the number of functioning parts. By (14), the value of having one less failed part decreases in the number of functioning parts. (15) does not have an intuitive interpretation; however, it resembles the concept ofK-convexity except that the value ofKhere varies iny. This can be seen by noting that (15) is equivalent tov(0,y−1)≤y−12y−1v(0,y−2)+y2y−1(v(0,y)+K(y)), whereK(y)≔h+bν1y. (16) unfolds the boundary property of the value function as the system state switches from the inventory region (with allmparts functioning) to the failure region (with failed parts that are waiting to be replaced). It shows that the value of having one spare part available when a failure occurs is more significant than that of having one unit of additional inventory. (17) implies that it is optimal to print whenever the part is in failure, and the cost reduction from having one less failed part is bounded by the product of a part’s expected lifetime (1/ν1) and a capped cost rate (h+b). (13) further leads to the optimality of a system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy. For exponentially distributed part lifetime(i.e.,k= 1),the optimal policy for the intelligent system is a stationary base-stock policy given by Note thatπm,1*(x,y)=1ify<mis implied byLemma 1. ByTheorem 1, with exponential lifetime, predictive print on demand is not necessarily optimal in general. Rather, when all parts in use are functioning, it is optimal to use predictive print to stock with a constant base-stock levelsm*. Predictive print on demand is optimal only whensm*=0, and therefore, its optimality depends on system parameters. Impact of m. As the system sizemgrows,sm*is nondecreasing, implying that the optimality of predictive print on demand will be less likely to hold. The rationale behind this is that the required inventory level cannot decrease when there are more machines instead of fewer machines. InSection 5.1, we confirm this insight for the upper-bound system with generalkphases of the part lifetime (Theorem 3). We will also examine the impact of other system parameters on the optimality of predictive print on demand inSection 7. So far, we have been focusing on cases with exponential part lifetimes. In these cases, the embedded sensor reading only provides binary information; the part is functioning or not, which may represent the situations where sensor estimates are too noisy to provide informative data on the residual lifetime of a part. To gain deeper understanding on the role of IoT, we next deviate from the exponential lifetime to model the situation where the embedded sensors are able to capture nontrivial residual lifetimes. For brevity, we only discuss the main results inSection 4.2.2and put the supporting derivations in Online Appendix A. 4.2.2. Single Machine with Two-Phase Part Lifetime. Consider the case ofm=1,k=2(i.e., a single machine and part lifetime with two phases). The state space in (3) isS1,2≔Z+×Z+2(1)withZ+2(1)={(0,0),(1,0),(0,1)}. By assuming that the back-order cost rate is sufficiently larger than the holding cost rate, we obtain the following results. Consider a single-machine system where the part lifetime has two phases(i.e.,m= 1,k= 2).If there isbh≥max{μν1+ν2+β,ν2β−1},the optimal policy for the intelligent system is a stationary system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy given by ByTheorem 2, it is again generally optimal to use predictive print to stock, and predictive print on demand is optimal only ifse1*=0(i.e., no inventory is held when the part in use is brand new, and a printing job is triggered only to fulfill an upcoming demand). Impact of k. As there is more than one phase in the part’s lifetime, the optimal base-stock level when the part in use is functioning depends on the system lifetime statusy(i.e., in which phase the functioning part is). This is in contrast to the exponential lifetime case inSection 4.2.1. In particular, the base-stock level when the functioning part’s lifetime is in phase 1 is no more than that when it is in phase 2. This suggests a positive role of IoT on inventory reduction. As a replacement demand becomes more imminent with the part’s aging, a predictive printing order may be needed. However, this change in the base-stock level is capped by one unit (i.e., at most one unit of predictive printing is needed during the part’s aging). InSection 5.1, we confirm this insight for the upper-bound system with general system sizemandkphases of part lifetime (Theorem 3). Unfortunately, we are not able to generalize the optimality proof to intelligent systems with largerk. The main challenge lies in the intractability resulting from the interdependence between inventory availability and replacement demands, which prevents us from showing the convexity property needed for the optimality of a base-stock policy. However, as illustrated inSection 5, the optimality can go through for general parameters when the intelligent system is slightly modified. In addition, the optimality is also verified in all numerical instances for the intelligent system inSection 7.
article.5. Upper-Bound System: In this section, we consider an artificial system, where everything stays the same as in the intelligent system except that spare parts can be immediately borrowed from an uncapacitated emergency source and need to be returned with printed parts whenever they are available. The same penalty cost ratebapplies to each borrowed part until being returned. The part borrowing feature enables the replacement demands to become exogenous and no longer depend on the availability of on-hand inventory. In particular, the resulting inventory level of spare parts, denoted asX˜(t), can drop below zero, representing back orders because of borrowed parts. Additionally, the resulting system lifetime status, denoted asY˜(t), evolves independently fromX˜(t). Consequently, it is a production-inventory system with full backlogging and exogenous demand arrival process. Withmmachines, the demand arrival is the superposition ofmindependent arrival processes, each with phase-type distributed interarrival times. As we will show inSection 5.2, this system provides an upper bound for the intelligent system in both optimal cost and base-stock levels; therefore, we name this system theupper-bound system. The state space of this system isS˜≔Z×Z+k(m)\Z+k(m−1). The system state is composed of the inventory levelX˜(t)∈Z, andY˜(t)=(Y˜1(t),…,Y˜k(t))∈Z+k(m)\Z+k(m−1), whereY˜i(t)is the number of parts whose lifetimes are in phasei. Note that∑i=1kY˜i(t)=mbecause there are alwaysmfunctioning parts with borrowing allowed. A deterministic stationary policyπ˜≔{π˜(x,y):(x,y)∈S˜}specifies the action taken at any time as a function of the system state only, whereπ˜(x,y)=1means to print in state(x,y)andπ˜(x,y)=0means not to print. Let the set of such printing policies be denoted byΠ˜. We will again work with a uniformized version of the problem. Use{(X˜n,Y˜n):n≥0}to denote the embedded Markov chain of states. If actiona∈{0,1}is selected in state(x,y), then the next state is(x′,y′)with probability Note that (20) is much simpler than (6). With parts borrowing allowed, there are no longer failed parts without replacement, and a replacement does not rely on the printing decision or inventory availability anymore. Moreover, in contrast to (4), the inventory holding and back-order cost rate now is given byc˜(x,y)≔h(x)++b(x)−, which only depends onx. Additionally, the value function is 5.1. Optimal Policy LetV˜be the set of real-valued functions onS˜, and letv˜be an arbitrary element ofV˜. DefineT˜i,T˜μ:V˜→V˜as follows: Consider also the operatorT˜:V˜→V˜defined by A detailed derivation of (25), interpretations of each term, and further details regarding the value functionv˜*are provided in Online Appendix A. We haveProposition 2. The value functionv˜*∈U˜≔{v˜∈V˜:v˜satisfiesproperties(26)–(29)},where the properties are given by In particular, (26) implies that when allmparts are working, the value of having one extra inventory decreases in the inventory level, which reflects a convexity property that can be used to show the existence of a system lifetime status-dependent base-stock optimal policy. (27) and (28) can be used to show that the update (aging) of a part in use will cause the base-stock level either to increase by one or to remain unchanged. (29) implies that it is optimal to print whenever there are backlogged parts (because of borrowing). LeveragingProposition 2, we can show the following results. In the upper-bound system,the optimal policy is a stationary system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy given by Theorem 3(a)confirms the intuition that it is always optimal to print whenever there are back orders. ByTheorem 3(b), the optimal system lifetime status-dependent base-stock level increases as a part ages, and such an increase is at most one unit. That is, the upcoming replacement demand because of a particular part’s aging becomes more imminent as the part approaches the end of its life cycle, and it triggers the potential need to predictively place a printing order. The total amount of such increase in base-stock level is capped bymas themparts in use evolve from all being brand new (phase 1) to all being about to fail (phasek), according toTheorem 3(c). This also demonstrates the easy-to-compute and nontrivial nature of our printing policy as the number of threshold is significantly less than the number of system states. On the other hand,Theorem 3, (b) and (c) also imply the potential reduction of the base-stock levels as the about-to-fail parts (in phasek) are replaced by new ones (in phase 1) after finishing their last phase. That is, the replaced parts will update the system lifetime status and hence, reduce the required inventory level. These observations collectively demonstrate how the system-level information enabled by IoT can be judiciously used to make the optimal predictive printing decisions. We will further illustrate this inExample 1ofSection 5.2. Lastly,Theorem 3(d)generalizes the monotonicity result inTheorem 1. The optimal base-stock levels increasing in the number of machines (shown for small intelligent systems) hold for an upper-bound system of any size. ApplyingTheorem 3to the single-machine case immediately leads to the following corollary, which indicates the existence of a threshold phasei*. When m= 1,there existsi*such thats˜ei*=s˜ei**fori≤i*ands˜ei*=s˜ei**+1fori>i*. ByCorollary 1, for a single-machine system, a print order should be placed whenever the part’s lifetime exceeds the threshold phasei*. Therefore, when adapted to the single-machine case where interconnection among parts is naturally absent, the optimal policy possesses a threshold structure that is consistent with the alert mechanism integrated into unconnected sensors. 5.2. Relationship with the Intelligent System In this subsection, we leverage the optimal policy for the upper-bound system to gain deeper understanding of the intelligent system. First, the following results demonstrate the upper-bounding relationship between the two systems in terms of optimal costs and base-stock levels. For any state(x,y)withx∈Z+,y∈Z+k(m)\Z+k(m−1),there are(a)v*(x,y)≤v˜*(x,y)and(b)sy*≤s˜y*. It is worth noting that the proof ofProposition 3does not require the phases of part lifetime to be exponentially distributed, suggesting the potential of applying our framework to a more general context. ByProposition 3(b),s˜y*provides upper bounds to the optimal base-stock levels in the intelligent system. To visualize this relationship and better understand how predictive 3DP is conducted according to the optimal policy, we consider the following numerical example and plot the optimal base-stock levels obtained from the two systems inFigure 6. Considerm=4,k=3,1/ν1=2.7weeks,1/ν2=5.4weeks,1/ν3=0.9weeks,h=$0.29/unit·week,b=$100/unit·week,1/μ=1week, andβ=0.5. InFigure 6, the optimal base-stock levels in the two systems are plotted against the system lifetime statusy, which is clustered into four groups. Fory∈G1, at least two parts are brand new (phase 1), and no parts are about to fail (phase 3); therefore, the optimal base-stock level for the intelligent system is the lowest withsy*=2. Fory∈G2, there is at least one part about to fail or at least three parts being moderately worn (phase 2), andsy*grows to three, resulting in a printing order placed. Fory∈G3, there are at least two parts about to fail or at least three parts being moderately worn, andsy*further increases to four, triggering another printing order predictively. Finally, fory∈G4, there are at least three parts about to fail and no brand new parts, andsy*reaches its maximum of five, dictating another printing order. The evolution of the optimal base-stock level demonstrates that the optimal policy not only depends on each individual part’s lifetime status but also, depends on the distribution of all parts’ lifetime status. In other words, it is the system-level criteria, rather than part-level criteria, that determine the optimal predictive printing decisions. Note also that the optimal base-stock levels of the intelligent system and the upper-bound system coincide in all states except for one. In the numerical study inSection 7, we continue to find them to be very close among all numerical instances. 5.2.1. Queueing Perspective for Single-Machine System. To further demonstrate that the upper-bound system can well approximate the intelligent system, we illustrate the two systems from a queueing perspective in this subsection. For simplicity, we consider the single-machine case (i.e.,m= 1) and implement astate-independentbase-stock policy for both systems. Additionally, we use the long-run average cost criteria of for queueing evaluations. Intelligent System. Under a given base-stock policys, the intelligent system is equivalent to aG/M/1/s+1queue with phase-type distributed interarrival times, exponential service time, and waiting room size ofs+ 1 (including the one in service). The spare parts demands are the arrivals, and the 3D printer is the server. When the part’s lifetime is exponentially distributed (k= 1), the intelligent system becomes anM/M/1/s+1queue with arrival rateν1. Additionally, it can be shown that (see Online Appendix A for details) the long-run average inventory holding and delay penalty cost isC(s)≔h(ρs+1−(s+2)ρ+s+1(1−ρ)(1−ρs+2)−1)+b·ρs+1−ρs+21−ρs+2,whereρ≔ν1/μis the utilization of this queueing system. AlthoughC(s) is not convex insin general, the following results hold. Combined with (31), this implies thats*depends on the cost parameters only throughb/hand is increasing inb/h. Upper-Bound System. Under a given base-stock policys˜, the upper-bound system can be viewed as aG/M/1queue with phase-type distributed interarrival times and exponential service time. Again, the spare parts demands are the arrivals and the 3D printer is the server. The only difference from the queue for the intelligent system is that there is no longer a waiting space limit because here, the spare parts demand is exogenous. When the part’s lifetime is exponentially distributed (k= 1), the upper-bound system is anM/M/1queue with arrival rateν1. article.6. Value of IoT: In this section, we discuss the value of IoT by comparing the intelligent system with the two benchmark systems described inSection 3.2.2. For the purpose of comparison, we consider the same initial state across systems. Hence, we will omit for brevity the initial states in all systems’ expected total discounted costs. Unlike in the intelligent system, the system lifetime status is not fully observable in the benchmark systems. It is well recognized that the optimal policies for partially observable Markov decision processes are difficult to find and compute (seeBertsekas 2012, pp. 351–352). Therefore, we consider commonly used, easy-to-implement heuristic policies for the benchmark systems based on the information structures they possess, with policy parameters optimized. As we will demonstrate inSections 6.1and6.2, these policies reduce to the optimal policy for the intelligent system when part lifetime is exponential, and they also include the policies considered in the literatures as special cases. We will define the metrics to quantify IoT’s value inSection 6.3. 6.1. Passive System In the passive system, the only available information is the number of functioning parts (i.e.,y¯) because of the lack of embedded sensors and IoT. To mark the notations that are affected because of such changes, we will add superscripto. In this system, the action space in (7) shrinks toΠo≡{π∈Π:π(x,y)=π(x,y′)foranyx∈Z+,y,y′∈Z+k(m)withy¯=y¯′}.We adopt a base-stock policy that depends on part failures (m−y¯) only: That is, for any state(x,y), ify¯<m, always print; ify¯=m, print up toso. Under this policy, every printing order is triggered by a part failure. Clearly,πo∈Πo. Letvodenote the expected total discounted cost underπo. Note that whenso=0, the policy reduces to print on demand. When the part lifetime is exponential withk= 1, the system lifetime status becomes one dimension withy¯=y, which contains no additional information other than the number of functioning parts. In this case, the passive system becomes the intelligent system as formally stated here. Whenk= 1 andsois optimally chosen,πocoincides with the optimal policy for the intelligent system. 6.2. Proactive System In the proactive system, the number of advance orders is also available. In other words, the number of functioning parts whose lifetimes are in or past phaseτis known. To mark the notations that are affected because of such changes, we will add superscripte. In this system, the action space in (7) is shrunken toΠe≡{π∈Π:π(x,y)=π(x,y′)foranyx∈Z+,yandy′∈Z+k(m)withy¯=y¯′andy¯T=y¯T′}, whereTandy¯Tare as defined inSection 3.2.2. Everything else stays the same as in the intelligent system. Note that there isΠo⊂Πe⊂Π. We adopt a base-stock policy that depends on part failures (m−y¯) as well as the number of advance orders (y¯T): Note that whenp>2m(i.e.,⌊y¯T/p⌉≡0), the policy reduces to the one in the passive system. This policy also includes the part age-based advance order policy inDeshpande et al. (2006)as a special case (seeDeshpande et al. 2006, section 4.3), although the context and methodology are different. When the part lifetime is exponential, the threshold phase becomes trivial withτ=k=1. Then, there are essentially noadvanceorders becausey¯T=y¯=y(i.e., there is no additional information than the number of functioning parts). In this case, the proactive system becomes equivalent to the passive system and hence, the intelligent system: Whenk= 1 andp,qare optimally chosen,πecoincides with the optimal policy for the intelligent system. 6.3. Metrics and Insights By the definitions inSections 6.1and6.2, there isvo≥ve≥v*. We can thus define the following cost improvements as values of IoT, advance information, and real-time information fusion, respectively: 6.3.1. Composition of IoT’s Value. Note thatVIoT=VAdI+VInF, where the first term is attributed to the advance demand information enabled by embedded sensors, whereas the second term is attributed to the real-time information fusion enabled by IoT’s continuous integration of the data from local sensors into system-level information. Note that the decomposed valuesVAdIandVInFcan be leveraged to compare against the investment costs of IoT/embedded sensors to assess whether it is worthwhile to invest in IoT or embedded sensors only. 6.3.2. Null Impact of IoT When Part Lifetime Is Exponential. ByRemarks 1and2, all the metrics are equal to zero whenk= 1 (i.e., part lifetime is exponential). This indicates that IoT’s positive impact on cost improvement hinges upon the effectiveness of embedded sensors in providing nontrivial estimates of the remaining part lifetime. If the sensor estimates are too noisy to provide informative data (either because of poor sensor quality or because of a high level of inherent variability in part lifetime), the introduction of IoT will be futile. InSection 7, we will apply the metrics in (35) and generate further insights on IoT’s value.
article.7. Numerical Study: In this section, we conduct a numerical study to complement our analytical results. First, we want to verify the optimality of the system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy for general intelligent systems, and we also assess the effectiveness of usings˜y*as a heuristic. Second, we want to examine when the optimal policy is predictive print on demand. Last but not least, we want to implement the metrics defined inSection 6.3and study how IoT’s value depends on system parameters. 7.1. Numerical Design Use one week as the time unit. We consider four levels for the system size:m=4,6,8,10. This range is consistent with the literature; the number of sensors connected in a local environment is typically limited because of constraints, such as bandwidth, energy consumption, etc. (seeSaghafian et al. 2021and the references therein). Consider two levels for the average lifetime of a part∑i=1k(1/νi): 9 and 18 (weeks; i.e., each part breaks every other month and every four months on average, respectively, representing parts with regular-moving and slow-moving demand rates). The number of lifetime phaseskis set to be three, representing like new (phase 1), moderately worn (phase 2), and abnormal/about to fail (phase 3), which on average, account for30%,60%,and 10% of a part’s lifetime, respectively. There are three levels of unit printing cost for the part, denoted asu:$100,$500, and$1,000, featuring cheap, medium, and expensive printing costs, respectively. Consider two levels of unit holding cost rate:h=15%·u/52and25%·u/52(per unit per week), representing 15% and 25% annual cost of capital because of interest, warehouse fee, parts deterioration, etc., respectively. There are two levels of penalty costs:b=$100,$500(per unit per week), representing the loss caused by a part not being replaced in a timely manner, such as facility downtime, etc. Three levels of printing speeds are considered:μ=1,2,6, representing 1 week (7 days), 0.5 weeks (3.5 days), and 0.17 weeks (1.2 days) of printing times, respectively. The speed settings here are comparable with those in a real-world case of an F-18 Super Hornet fighter jet, whose parts are 3D printed (Khajavi et al. 2014). We consider all 288 combinations of the parameter choices (Table 1). Table 1.Numerical Design Table 1.Numerical Design For each instance, we first calculate the optimal policies of the intelligent system and the upper-bound system,π*andπ˜*, via value iterations for the discounted dynamic programming with the discount factorβ=0.5.1We next calculate the policies for the passive system and the proactive system,πoandπeas defined in (33) and (34), respectively, by optimally choosing the parameterssoandp,q. For the proactive system,τis set as three (i.e., an advance order is placed whenever the lifetime of a part enters the abnormal/about-to-fail phase). (In Online Appendix A, we examine the alternate case ofτbeing two (i.e., the moderately worn phase).) Lastly, we assess the expected total discounted cost of the aforementioned four policies when they are implemented in the intelligent system. For the purpose of comparison, we consider the same initial state across systems with zero inventory and all parts in use being brand new (i.e., in phase 1 of their lifetimes). Again, we omit for brevity the initial states in all systems’ expected total discounted costs. Specifically, we calculatev*,vπ˜*,vo, andve, wherevπ˜*denotes the total discounted cost whenπ˜*is used as a heuristic for the intelligent system. To assess the effectiveness ofπ˜*as a heuristic, we calculate the following metric of the relative cost error for each instance:AvgErr≔vπ˜*−v*v*×100%.To examine whenπ*is predictive print on demand, we count how often it happens by reporting the percentage of its occurrence among all numerical instances as well as instance subsets of system parameters. We further calculate the metrics defined in (35), and we report the average values of these metrics across all numerical instances as well as instance subsets of system parameters. The numerical results are summarized inTables 2and3. Table 2.Statistics of Numerical Results Table 2.Statistics of Numerical Results Table 3.Numerical Results for Instance Subsets Table 3.Numerical Results for Instance Subsets Note. PPOD, predictive print on demand. 7.2. Optimal Policy and Heuristic Performance Inallinstances,π*is found to be a system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy, confirming the structure of the optimal policy that we have shown for special cases of intelligent systems (Section 4.2) as well as the general case of the upper-bound system (Section 5.1).Table 2demonstrates a very high accuracy ofπ˜*as a heuristic, with the maximal relative cost error of 0.89%. Moreover, in 78.82% of all instances, the heuristic is exactly optimal. 7.3. Predictive Print on Demand Becauseπ*is a system lifetime status-dependent base-stock policy in all instances, it is predictive print on demand whensy*satisfiesminy∈Z+k(m)\Z+k(m−1){sy*}=0.Otherwise, it is predictive print to stock. 7.3.1. Printing Speed Is the Dominating Factor. ByTable 3, the dominating factor among all parameters is the printing speedμ. Its different values contribute to a drastic variation in the occurrence of predictive print on demand as optimal; it varies from 16.67% to 92.71% as the unit printing time decreases from one week to 1.2 days. In contrast, the second most significant factor is the unit printing cost; as it increases from $100 to $1,000, the range of variation for the occurrence is much narrower from 31.25% to 68.75%. This suggests that the optimality of predictive print on demand heavily relies on the acceleration of 3DP. Otherwise, regular inventory is still a necessity for predictive 3DP. To visualize this impact, we revisitExample 1by increasing the printing speed without altering other parameters. More numerical results on how these two factors jointly influence the system cost can be found in Online Appendix A. Consider the same parameters as inExample 1except for reduction in the average printing time from 1 week (7 days) to 0.17 weeks (∼1.2 days). InFigure 7, the optimal base-stock levels from the intelligent system and the upper-bound system are plotted against the system lifetime statusy. Note that the optimal base-stocks levels from the two systems completely coincide. Moreover, unlike inExample 1, the optimal policy here is predictive print on demand; when all four parts are brand new in phase 1 (i.e.,y=(4,0,0)), a zero base-stock level suffices. As the parts age, the optimal base-stock level increases gradually from zero to four, triggering printing jobs to fulfill upcoming demands. 7.4. Value of IoT Table 2reveals that IoT has significant value by leading to relative cost reductions (VIoT) that can be as high as 77.40% and 24.56% on average. 7.4.1. IoT’s Value Does Not Necessarily Scale Up. Table 3shows that the subgroup average of IoT’s value decreases in the number of machinesm. This suggests that caution is needed when executing the conventional wisdom that IoT should be more valuable for larger systems. The seemingly counterintuitive finding is driven by the fact that expandingmwithout pairing it with appropriate printing capacity may inadvertently demolish IoT’s value. This can also be seen from the decreasing pattern displayed in the occurrence of predictive print on demand being optimal asmincreases; with more machines to serve, the printer utilization increases, and the printing capacity can become a bottleneck to prevent 3DP from flexibly leveraging the information from IoT, resulting in higher dependency on inventory. We further elaborate on the insight using instance-level numerical results. Consider the same setting as inExample 1except that we vary1/μfrom 1 week to 0.17 weeks and varymfrom 4 to 10 as specified inTable 1. For the different combinations of1/μandm, the resulting IoT’s value is plotted inFigure 8, where it increases only as the number of machines expands from four to six with1/μ= 0.17 weeks. This means that, even with a fast printer, any expansion beyond six machines requires more printers, with a desired machine-to-printer ratio of six to one. For slower printers, printing capacity bottlenecks the scaling of IoT’s value even with only four machines. Hence, to achieve the scaling of IoT’s value, the system should expand in both the number of machines and the number of printers, with a desired machine-to-printer ratio of no more than four to one. 7.4.2. Value of Advance Information Is Predominant. We observe a consistent pattern in the composition of IoT’s value across all instance subsets; the portion attributed to advance demand information (VAdI) is always predominant compared with that of real-time information fusion (VInF). It is worth mentioning that this observation continues to hold even when the proactive system performs in an inferior way (see Online Appendix A).
article.8. Conclusion and Discussion: We present a versatile framework that examines predictive 3DP in the context of spare parts supply. Our approach captures the operational characteristics of 3DP and crucial features of IoT, revealing the optimal predictive printing policy and advancing the theory for production-inventory systems with endogenous demand. Although the consensus may suggest that the combination of 3DP and IoT would bring on-demand printing to fruition, our findings urge caution before jumping to conclusions. The real advantage of this combination is to enable predictive print to stock by judiciously using the system-level information that IoT provides. Whether the optimal policy leads to minimal inventory through predictive print on demand hinges on system parameters, particularly the 3DP speed. We develop benchmark systems and metrics to gauge the value of IoT in cost reductions and decompose it into two portions: one attributed to embedded sensors’ advance information and the other attributed to IoT’s real-time information fusion. We apply the metrics in a numerical study and find that IoT delivers significant value in general. However, the notion that IoT’s value scales up for larger systems is suitable only when the expansion is paired with appropriate 3DP capacity. Our framework can help spare parts suppliers evaluate their investment decisions on IoT/embedded sensors and develop predictive printing scheduling tools. Our framework is applicable to the multiprinter scenario where each replacement demand is randomly assigned among all printers with equal likelihood. Additionally, our model can handle multiprinter parts and multiple types of parts as long as each printer is responsible for a single part type. When each printer is allowed to print multiple part types, the IoT’s value quantified through our framework (by restricting each printer with one part type) provides a lower bound for the actual value brought by IoT because of the additional flexibility in printing capacity. Furthermore, our framework is not limited to the application in spare parts and can be adapted for application in other fields. For example, it can be used to model bioprinting—it creates live organs or tissues using inkjet, extrusion, laser direct writing, or light-assisted 3DP techniques (Hanaphy 2020b); it can also 3D print sensors onto expanding organs that helps medical staff monitor the conditions of patients (Hanaphy 2020a). Because bioprinting typically involves perishable and personalized materials, it is conducted either on demand or in a predictive manner based on the patient’s medical condition or projected schedules of surgical procedures. Our framework can be adjusted to reflect the specific healthcare context and help with predictive printing scheduling in bioprinting applications.

AItitle: 工业4.0生产革命：物联网驱动3D打印预测性备货最优解
AIsummary: 这篇论文聚焦工业4.0中的物联网（IoT）与3D打印（3DP）技术融合，探索如何通过实时数据优化关键备件的预测性生产调度。传统研究多孤立分析传感器监测或按需打印策略，但未能解决两者协同下的动态决策难题——例如，现有文献假设需求外生或忽略打印容量限制，导致实际应用中库存与响应效率失衡。作者提出创新性的“智能系统”模型：通过IoT实时监控多设备零件老化状态，建立基于系统寿命状态的动态基准库存策略。研究发现，最优策略并非单纯按需打印，而是根据设备整体健康度预测性备货（print to stock），仅当3D打印速度足够快时，零库存的预测性按需模式（predictive print on demand）才适用。研究还通过构建“被动系统”（无IoT）和“主动系统”（仅局部传感器）作为对照组，量化了IoT的价值——其核心贡献60%来自传感器提供的预警信息，40%源于IoT实现的全局数据融合。数值实验表明，3D打印速度是决定模式选择的首要因素（速度提升可使预测按需模式适用性从16.7%跃升至92.7%），而盲目扩大监控规模若不匹配打印容量，反而削弱IoT效益。该框架为制造业投资IoT/传感器提供了量化决策工具，并可扩展至生物打印等实时响应场景。
AIkeys: 嵌入式传感器预警机制
Highlights:
学术要点 1：预测性按需打印（Predictive Print on Demand）|||
一种基于系统状态的最小库存打印模式，通过物联网实时信息提前触发打印任务以匹配即将产生的备件需求。|||
学术要点 2：系统生命周期状态（System Lifetime Status）|||
通过物联网融合多设备传感器数据形成的多维状态向量，表征所有在用零件的寿命阶段分布，作为预测性打印决策的核心依据。|||
学术要点 3：内生需求动态（Endogenous Demand Dynamics）|||
备件需求与库存可用性之间的双向耦合关系：设备故障产生的需求直接影响打印决策，而打印策略又反作用于设备运行状态。|||
学术要点 4：上界系统框架（Upper-Bound System Framework）|||
通过引入应急备件借贷机制构建的分析模型，将内生需求转化为外生需求流，为复杂生产-库存系统提供策略结构解析方法。|||
学术要点 5：信息结构价值分解（Value Decomposition of Information Structures）|||
通过基准系统对比量化物联网价值，分离嵌入式传感器的预先信息价值与物联网实时信息融合价值，揭示不同信息维度的成本节约机制。

图片展示了3D打印机与物联网设备协同实现预测性制造的系统架构。
示意图展示了3D打印机通过物联网监测设备状态进行预测性补货的运作模式。
图片展示了基于物联网的3D打印生产系统模型，涉及库存阈值与多设备协同机制。
图片展示了物联网连接的3D打印机与生产设备间的协同运作模式，重点呈现预测性打印与实时信息融合的机制。
该图展示了基于物联网监测的3D打印预测性生产系统模型。
